chunk_summary: Guidelines for initiating, composing, monitoring, and weaning parenteral nutrition (PN) in chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing safety protocols, refeeding risk, and ICU coordination.
learning_objectives: Understand indications for initiating parenteral nutrition in chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Learn strategies to mitigate refeeding syndrome during PN initiation.
Identify key monitoring parameters for safe PN administration in ICU settings.
Recognize the importance of tailoring PN composition to respiratory and fluid management needs.
key_facts: PN is indicated when enteral intake is <60–75% of targets for >48–72 hours and recovery is unlikely within the next day.
Baseline phosphate, magnesium, and thiamine are critical in children with refeeding risk before significant calorie delivery.
Daily monitoring of glucose, electrolytes, phosphate, and magnesium is essential during the first 72 hours of PN initiation.
Excessive dextrose in PN can increase CO2 production, which is detrimental in respiratory disease.
PN fluid volume must align with the cardiopulmonary plan to avoid excess fluid burden.
Central line care bundles are mandatory to prevent catheter-related infections.
reference_quote: PN is indicated when enteral feeding is not feasible, not safe, or cannot meet minimum requirements despite a structured enteral strategy. (Mehta et al.)
document_key_topics: Nutritional strategies in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
